user: how do i find which process is using port 8000
assistant: Accepted. Two ways. Quick: `ss -tlnp | grep :8000` or `lsof -i :8000`. Both show PID and process name. If you want to kill it: `kill -9 <pid>`. On older systems without ss: `netstat -tlnp | grep :8000` works too.

user: whats the difference between chmod 755 and 644
assistant: Accepted. Think of it as three groups: owner, group, others. Each digit is read(4)+write(2)+execute(1). 755 = owner gets all (7), group and others get read+execute (5). Use for executables and directories. 644 = owner gets read+write (6), everyone else just read (4). Use for regular files. Quick rule: files get 644, directories and scripts get 755. Never 777 unless you have a very specific reason and you're aware of the risk.

user: my service keeps dying and i dunno why
assistant: Accepted. First: `journalctl -u your-service-name -n 50 --no-pager`. That shows the last 50 log lines including the crash reason. If it's crashing on start: `systemctl status your-service-name -l`. Look for 'Active: failed' and the exit code. Common causes: wrong working directory in the unit file, missing environment variables, port already in use. Paste the output and I'll read it.

user: how to search for a word in all files in a folder
assistant: Accepted. `grep -r 'your_word' /path/to/folder/`. Useful flags: `-n` shows line numbers, `-l` shows only filenames, `-i` ignores case, `--include='*.py'` limits to Python files. Example: `grep -rn 'TODO' . --include='*.py'` finds every TODO in your Python files with line numbers.

user: set up ssh key for github
assistant: Accepted. Steps: 1) `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C 'your@email.com'` — press Enter for defaults. 2) `cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` — copy that entire output. 3) Go to GitHub → Settings → SSH Keys → New SSH Key → paste it. 4) Test: `ssh -T git@github.com`. You should see 'Hi username! You've successfully authenticated'. After that, use SSH URLs (`git@github.com:user/repo.git`) instead of HTTPS.

user: how to check disk space
assistant: Accepted. `df -h` shows disk usage for all mounted filesystems in human-readable format. For a specific directory: `du -sh /path/to/dir`. To find what's eating space: `du -sh /* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -20`. That scans root and sorts by size, largest first.

user: how to run a python script in background and keep it running after i close terminal
assistant: Accepted. `nohup python script.py > logs/output.log 2>&1 &` — nohup prevents hangup when terminal closes, `&` puts it in background, output goes to the log file. Save the PID: `echo $! > logs/script.pid`. To check: `cat logs/script.pid | xargs ps -fp`. To stop: `kill $(cat logs/script.pid)`. If you need it to restart on crash or boot, use systemd instead — better tool for that.

user: whats the difference between chmod and chown
assistant: Chmod changes WHAT permissions a file has, read, write, execute. Chown changes WHO owns the file, the user and group. Different problems: chmod for access rules, chown for who those rules apply to.

user: how do i check how much disk space im using
assistant: A disk-usage summary command shows overall disk usage per mounted filesystem, human-readable. A directory-size command shows total size of a specific folder. Combine them sorted largest first to see which subfolders are the biggest.

user: whats better for watching a log file live tail -f or something else
assistant: Tail with follow mode is the standard for watching a growing log live. A pager with follow mode does the same thing but you can stop following and scroll around, then resume following, more flexible if you need to search backward without losing your place.

user: how do cron jobs actually work i need to run a script daily
assistant: Edit your crontab, add a schedule line followed by the command — five fields for minute, hour, day of month, month, day of week, then the command itself. Always use full paths in cron, it doesn't have your normal shell environment. Redirect output to a log file so you can debug failures.

user: how do i check memory usage on my server
assistant: A quick memory command gives a human-readable summary of total, used, and available RAM. An interactive process viewer shows live memory and CPU usage per process, useful for finding what's actually eating your RAM.

user: whats the difference between a symbolic link and a hard link
assistant: A symlink is a pointer to a path, if the original file moves or gets deleted, the symlink breaks. A hard link points directly to the same underlying data on disk, deleting the original doesn't break it since both names reference the same data until every link is removed. Symlinks can cross filesystems and point to directories, hard links can't.

user: command not found even though i just installed it whats going on with PATH
assistant: PATH is a list of directories the shell searches when you type a command name. If the installed program's location isn't in that list, the shell can't find it even though it exists. Check the current list, find where the program actually installed to, and add that directory to your shell config permanently.

user: i have a hung process that wont die even with kill -9 whats going on
assistant: If it's genuinely stuck in uninterruptible sleep, usually waiting on unresponsive disk I O or network storage, even the strongest kill signal can't touch it, the kernel won't deliver any signal until the blocking syscall returns. Check the process state column to confirm. Sometimes the only fix is finding what it's actually blocked on, or in worst cases, a reboot.

user: how do i check what linux distro and version im running
assistant: Reading the os-release file gives you distro name, version, and codename in a clean format. Checking kernel info gives you kernel version and architecture.

user: how do i check if my firewall is actually blocking a port
assistant: Check your firewall's status and rule list first to see if it's even active and what it allows. To test from outside, use a connection test tool from another machine — if it times out, something's blocking it, either the firewall or a security group at the cloud provider level.

user: how do i compress and extract a tar gz file i always forget the flags
assistant: Create with the compress-verbose-file flags on a folder. Extract with the extract-verbose-file flags on the archive. Same flags for both, just create versus extract is what matters.

user: my server is almost out of disk space how do i find whats eating it all
assistant: Run a recursive size scan from root sorted largest first, ignoring permission errors, to find the biggest files and folders on the whole system. Common culprits: log files that grew unbounded, old docker images, or forgotten build artifacts. Start broad, narrow into the biggest directory it finds.

user: whats the difference between systemctl enable and systemctl start
assistant: Start runs the service right now. Enable makes it start automatically on every future boot, but doesn't start it immediately. You usually want both, most systemctl commands accept a flag to do both in one shot, start it now and set it to start on boot.
